6.anchor: The complex was three stories high, the bottom floor being 7-1/2 feet wide, the second floor 9 feet wide, and the top floor 10-1/2 feet wide.
6.note: Hebrew the bottom floor being 5 cubits wide, the second floor 6 cubits wide, and the top floor 7 cubits wide

8.anchor: The entrance to the bottom floor

10.anchor: As already stated, he built a complex of rooms along the sides of the building, attached to the Temple walls by cedar timbers. Each story of the complex was 7-1/2 feet

17.anchor: The main room of the Temple, outside the Most Holy Place, was 60 feet

20.anchor: This inner sanctuary was 30 feet long, 30 feet wide, and 30 feet high. He overlaid the inside with solid gold. He also overlaid the altar made of cedar.
20.note: Or overlaid the altar with cedar.

21.anchor: Then Solomon overlaid the rest of the Temple’s interior with solid gold, and he made gold chains to protect the entrance

23.anchor: He made two cherubim of wild olive

24.anchor: The wingspan of each of the cherubim was 15 feet, each wing being 7-1/2 feet

31.anchor: For the entrance to the inner sanctuary, he made double doors of wild olive wood with five-sided doorposts.
31.note: The meaning of the Hebrew is uncertain.

38.anchor: The entire building was completed in every detail by midautumn, in the month of Bul,

5.anchor: “And there is something else. You know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me when he murdered my two army commanders, Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether. He pretended that it was an act of war, but it was done in a time of peace,
5.note: Or He murdered them during a time of peace as revenge for deaths they had caused in time of war.

5.anchor: staining his belt and sandals with innocent blood.
5.note: As in some Greek and Old Latin manuscripts; Hebrew reads with the blood of war.

6.anchor: Do with him what you think best, but don’t let him grow old and go to his grave in peace.
6.note: Hebrew don’t let his white head go down to Sheol in peace.

9.anchor: But that oath does not make him innocent. You are a wise man, and you will know how to arrange a bloody death for him.
9.note: Hebrew how to bring his white head down to Sheol in blood.

32.note: Hebrew will return his blood on his own head.

2.anchor: So all the men of Israel assembled before King Solomon at the annual Festival of Shelters, which is held in early autumn in the month of Ethanim.
2.note: Hebrew at the festival in the month Ethanim, which is the seventh month.

9.anchor: Nothing was in the Ark except the two stone tablets that Moses had placed in it at Mount Sinai,

13.anchor: Now I have built a glorious Temple for you, a place where you can live forever!

65.anchor: Then Solomon and all Israel celebrated the Festival of Shelters

our God. A large congregation had gathered from as far away as Lebo-hamath in the north and the Brook of Egypt in the south. The celebration went on for fourteen days in all—seven days for the dedication of the altar and seven days for the Festival of Shelters.
65.note: Hebrew seven days and seven days, fourteen days;

2.anchor: One of Solomon’s buildings was called the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon. It was 150 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high.
2.note: Hebrew 100 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high.

3.anchor: The hall had a cedar roof. Above the beams on the pillars were forty-five side rooms,

6.anchor: Solomon also built the Hall of Pillars, which was 75 feet long and 45 feet wide.
6.note: Hebrew 50 cubits long and 30 cubits wide.

7.anchor: Solomon also built the throne room, known as the Hall of Justice, where he sat to hear legal matters. It was paneled with cedar from floor to ceiling.
7.note: As in Syriac version and Latin Vulgate; Hebrew reads from floor to floor.

10.anchor: Some of the huge foundation stones were 15 feet long, and some were 12 feet

13.anchor: King Solomon then asked for a man named Huram

15.anchor: Huram cast two bronze pillars, each 27 feet tall and 18 feet in circumference.
15.note: Hebrew 18 cubits tall and 12 cubits in circumference.

16.anchor: For the tops of the pillars he cast bronze capitals, each 7-1/2 feet

19.anchor: The capitals on the columns inside the entry room were shaped like water lilies, and they were six feet

21.anchor: Huram set the pillars at the entrance of the Temple, one toward the south and one toward the north. He named the one on the south Jakin, and the one on the north Boaz.

23.anchor: Then Huram cast a great round basin, 15 feet across from rim to rim, called the Sea. It was 7-1/2 feet deep and about 45 feet in circumference.
23.note: Hebrew 10 cubits across. . . . 5 cubits deep and 30 cubits in circumference.

24.anchor: It was encircled just below its rim by two rows of decorative gourds. There were about six gourds per foot
24.note: Or 20 gourds per meter; 10 per cubit.

25.anchor: The Sea was placed on a base of twelve bronze oxen,

26.anchor: The walls of the Sea were about three inches
thick, and its rim flared out like a cup and resembled a water lily blossom. It could hold about 11,000 gallons

27.anchor: Huram also made ten bronze water carts, each 6 feet long, 6 feet wide, and 4-1/2 feet tall.
27.note: Hebrew 4 cubits long, 4 cubits wide, and 3 cubits high.

31.anchor: The top of each cart had a rounded frame for the basin. It projected 1-1/2 feet
above the cart’s top like a round pedestal, and its opening was 2-1/4 feet

35.anchor: Around the top of each cart was a rim nine inches wide.

38.anchor: Huram also made ten smaller bronze basins, one for each cart. Each basin was six feet across and could hold 220 gallons
